items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.q: What did Pollock mean by his drip paintings being 'in the painting'?
items.correct: He worked from all sides, making the act of painting central to the meaning.
items.misconception: He literally fell into wet paint.
items.why: Students take 'in the painting' literally. Pollock meant his BODILY ENGAGEMENT - working around and over the canvas on the floor - was integral to the work, not just the marks left behind.

items.q: Why is preserving white in watercolour important?
items.correct: White comes from the paper showing through - there is no true white pigment in traditional watercolour.
items.misconception: You can simply paint white over other colours later.
items.random_wrong: Because white is the most expensive colour.
items.why: Students plan to add white at the end as in opaque media. Watercolour is transparent - highlights must be PRESERVED from the start by leaving paper untouched or using masking fluid.

items.q: Who created the 'Angel of the North' sculpture installed in 1998?
items.why: Correct answer: Antony Gormley. “Anish Kapoor” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 'Infinity Rooms' are famous immersive installations?
items.why: It's Yayoi Kusama. “Olafur Eliasson” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Gormley's 'Angel of the North' installed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1998. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
